1: si: although this word appears second in the clause, it subordinates the entire couplet and should be read first. plorauit: recycle the verb for both occasions of the nominative mater. The unnamed mothers are different women. Memnona: accusative form of the Greek name Memnon (gen. Memnonis). Memnon, the son of Aurora and Tithonus, led the Aethiopians to fight in defense of Troy and died at Achilles’ hand, thus prompting his divine mother’s tears. mater plorauit Achillem: the mother of Achilles was Thetis, a sea nymph. Following mention of Aurora’s lament for Memnon, the implied occasion for Thetis’s lament is the death of Achilles, but Thetis is also described in the Iliad as weeping at the moment she realizes that he will note return from Troy (18.94-96).
2: This verse is still part of the protasis of a conditional sentence marked by si in verse 1.
3: flebilis: vocative and modifying Elegia, but it can be read adverbially with solue. solue capillos: a gesture associated with funereal grieving. Cf. Fasti 3.213-214.
4: ex uero = “from fact” or, more colloquially, “too true” or “accurate.” One of the proposed ancient etymologies of elegia derived the genre’s name from elegos (ἔλεγος), a Greek song of lament. tibi: dative of possessor.
5: ille … uates, tua fama: in apposition with Tibullus but suggesting that Tibullus is the source of Elegy’s fame.
6: corpus inane: also in apposition with Tibullus. The poet’s body is “empty” because it is no longer animated by his living spirit.
7: puer Veneris: Cupid. euersamque pharetram: That his quiver is upside down suggests the boy is disheveled. Note the polysyndeton.
8: fractos arcus et sine luce facem: additional markers of Cupid’s grief. He neglects the objects with which he is typically associated.
9: aspice: the speaker seems to be addressing the reader, but the singular imperative permits Elegia to be the addressee. ut eat: an indirect question with exclamatory force. See Allen and Greenough 573-574. miserabilis: an adjective functioning adverbially. Note the sonic near-echo of reading alīs immediately after this word.
10: infesta: scansion shows this to be ablative and so modifying manu. tundat: subjunctive because still in the indirect question introduced by ut in verse 9.
12: ora: from os, oris, n. Poets often used the plural form to express the singular.
13: An indirect statement introduced by dicunt with no expressed subject, akin to the modern phrase, “They say…” Augustan poets/speakers frequently demonstrate familiarity with other poetic traditions using this or similar constructions (e.g. sic fatur, ferunt) resulting in what scholars often call “Alexandrian footnotes.” Because Aeneas still lives at the end of the Aeneid, the tradition to which this couplet points is not Vergilian. The Latin word order is challenging, perhaps in an effort to represent Cupid’s dishevelment. Rendering the couplet in English word order might result in dicunt illum sic egressum (esse) tuis tectis,pulcher Iule, in funere fratris Aeneae. fratris: because Venus was also Aeneas’s mother, Cupid and Aeneas are here referred to as siblings. Cf. Amores 1.2 #checkverse for a similarly playful allusion to the familial relationship between Cupid and Augustus because of the Julian clans claim of descent from Aeneas. illum: an accusative subject of the indirect statement, the referent for which is still Cupid.
14: egressum: supply a form of esse tectis, pulcher Iule, tuis: in Vergil’s Aeneid, Iulus is another name for Ascanius, Aeneas’ son. After the death Aeneas, Iulus could rightly be regarded as in possession of his own house.
15: nec minus…/quam…cum: the comparison provides the underlying syntactical structure of the couplet. Note the sonic repetition of -us in the first half of this verse.
16: iuueni: a dative with inguen expressing possession and disadvantage. #check The youth is Adonis, a mortal lover of Venus known for his outstanding beauty, who died after being gored by a wild boar. Ovid embeds a version of Adonis’s conception, life, and death in Orpheus’s song (for whom, see the following notes on vv. 21-22) in Metamorphoses Book 10.
17: cura: a predicate nominative after uocamur. One might also read sacri as a predicate adjective after uocamur, thus modifying uates: “We bards are called sacred and the concern…” diuum: a genitive plural, as if syncopated from diuorum. Cf. Catullus c. 64.28.
18: putent: subjunctive in a relative clause of characteristic introduced but sunt qui. (A&G 535a). This verb introduces an indirect statement within the relative clause. Cf. Cic. Pro arch. 18-19 for a similar sentiment about the sacred, inspired, and quasi-divine nature of poets.
19: mors: Death personified, profaning even the sacred, comes for everyone. Cf. Horace. Odes 1.4.13-14 Pallida Mors aequo pulsat pede pauperum tabernas / regumque turris.
21: quid … profuit = “What did [nominative subject] benefit [dative object].” We might translate quid as how for more natural English. Ismario … Orpheo: dative form of Orpheus, a famed musician said to have sung near Mt. Ismarus, a mountain in Thrace. His poetry was so arresting that he could enchant animals. Cf. Ver. Ecl. 6.25. Roman mosaics frequently depict him seated with a lyre and surrounded by animals. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orpheus_mosaic#/media/File:DSC00355_-_Orfeo_(epoca_romana)_-_Foto_G._Dall'Orto.jpg Some traditions represent the mortal Oeagrus, a Thracian king, as Orpheus’s father, but the context here seems to refer to a tradition in which Apollo was his father and the muse Calliope his mother. The implication is that his divine parentage did not benefit him. Note the similarity between this verse and the opening verse of the poem where divine parentage is also mentioned.
22: quid: the syntax from the previous verse carries over, with profuit and Ismario Orpheo still implied. The new subject, however, is the infinitive obstipuisse, which takes uictas … feras as its direct object. carmine: an ablative of means, more smoothly read with uictas than the infinitive.
23: Linon: accusative form of the Greek name Linos (or Linus in Latin). Another famed musician, Linus is sometimes ascribed the same genealogy as Orpheus, thus making them brothers. Because his father is here described as playing the lyre in mourning (v. 24), we may assume that Apollo—“the same father” (idem pater)—is meant. aelinon: an aelinos or ailinos, from the Greek αἰλινος, meaning “an anguished cry; dirge; lament” is here etymologized as deriving from the exclamation “Alas for Linos!”, but others believe the name Linus was invented to explain the exclamation (OLD sv Linus)
24: inuita: a transferred epithet; the lyre rather than the singer is described as “unwilling.” #crossreference transferred epithet? The scribe of Manuscript P writes inuicta, #IIIF perhaps alluding to a tradition in which Apollo and Linus competed in a musical contest; Apollo, after slaying Linus, would have been victor by default, if not by judgment. If this version of the story is being invoked, the et with which the verse 23 begins could be rendered as “even” (rather than “also”) to highlight that Apollo lamented his son despite killing him.
25: Adice: another singular imperative, addressing either Elegia or the readership. The speaker adds to his list of bards who were not spared death. Maeoniden: accusative form of the Greek derived Maeonides, a toponym denoting someone from the region of Lydia. “The Maeonian” is often, as here, used to designate Homer. a quo: the preposition aids in determining that the relative pronoun is an ablative of agent.
26: Pieriis … aquis: the Pierian Spring, associated with the Muses, was believed to inspire those who drank from it. Cf. Am. 1.1.6: Pieridum uates … sumus (“We poets belong to the Muses…”). ora: see note on verse 12.
27: hunc: the antecedent is Maeoniden in verse 25. Auerno: Avernus is a lake near Puteoli (close to the Bay of Naples) associated with the entrance to the underworld.
28: defugiunt … carmina: the speaker distinguishes between poets and the creations that outlive them. For the reader of these verses, the observation comes with an implicit acknowledgement of the material forms in which poetry endures. Poems survive in a literary society when they are written down. rogos: There is some irony in the fact that fire poses a singular risk to most forms in which literature is preserved (especially to papyrus or wax tablets).
29: durat: the sense of this verb is applied three times in this and the following verse. Troiani fama laboris: a reference to Homer’s Iliad set in Troy. The scribe of Manuscript P #IIIF seems to have written durus op at uatum, which is unintelligible. Some sense could be derived from durus optat uatum, if a faint “t” in the middle of op at is actually present (e.g., “The harsh one of the poets wishes”), although how this would accord with the meaning of the rest of the verse is unclear. A second hand has corrected durus to durat and seems to have left some indication that the -us at the end of durus may belong where at appears, placing two dots under all four letters. Switching the locations of these letters gives us the text printed in Kenney.
30: tela retexta: a reference to Homer’s Odyssey, in which Penelope postponed her consideration of remarriage to the suitors on Ithaca until she completed weaving a funeral shroud for Odysseus’s father Laertes—a shroud she would unweave each night, rendering its progress tarda. The speaker fittingly refers to the Odyssey with a story concerned with textiles and materiality (i.e., retexta).
31: Nemesis … Delia: the names of two women whom Tibullus addresses in his poetic collections. Delia’s name appears frequently in the first half of Tibullus’s first book of elegies, and Nemesis occupies the speaker’s attention in the second book. The speaker here seems to be playing with the tradition of referring to an elegiac corpus using the name of the love object.
32: Note how the speaker neatly balances the pentameter and places the description of each woman beneath her name in verse 31.
33: uos: the speaker addresses both women, although the references in this couplet are drawn from a poem in the book in which Delia features, especially 1.3.23-26. The poem presents the sickly speaker, self-identified as “Tibullus,” contemplating his own death on the island of Phaeacia after having accompanied Messalla, presumably on a military expedition. The specific verses referenced in Ovid’s couplet echo a passage in which the Tibullan speaker recalls the efforts Delia undertook to ensure his safe return when he first set out: --- quid tua nunc Isis mihi, Delia, quid mihi prosunt      illa tua totiens aera repulsa manu, quidue, pie dum sacra colis, pureque lauari      te—memini—et puro secubuisse toro? --- “How now does your Isis, Delia, benefit me, how do those bronze rattles so often shaken in your hand, or how, while you piously perform sacred rites, that you are washed pure—I recall—and that you sleep apart in a pure bed? --- The Ovidian speaker recalls these verses as he asks the woman what benefit these actions brought Tibullus, now that he has died. quid … prosunt: Note the speaker’s use of the same construction as Tibullus, including his repetition of several of the same words and syntxes in this and the following verse.
34: sistra: the sistrum is a rattle associated with the Egyptian goddess Isis whose worship was often performed in mystery cults popular with women. secubuisse: sleeping separately implies abstinence, presumably a requisite part of a woman’s worship of Isis.
35: ignoscite: a plural imperative that here takes a dative object (fasso). The speaker may still be addressing Delia and Nemesis. fasso: a substantive adjective from the participle of fateor = “the thing having been confessed/declared” or “the declaration.” The force seems similar to the modern parenthetical, “I’m sorry to say (but…),” followed, as here, by a potentially controversial confession.
36: sollicitor: sollicitāre can mean “to tempt” when paired with an infinitive (OLD s.v. sollicitō 6b).
37: uiue … cole: singular imperatives. The speaker addresses a rhetorical “you.” pius: a nominative adjective with adverbial force. moriere: an alternative form for the second person singular morieris. Scansion shows this deponent verb to be in the future (moriēre). colentem: a substantive adjective from the participle of colō = “the one performing [sacred rites].”
38: a templis: death (Mors) does not spare the devout. The enjambment of this and the previous verse highlights the suddenness with which death can change someone’s circumstances.
39: confido: governs its objects in the dative. Using Tibullus as proof, the speaker again emphasizes that faith in good poetry will not spare one from death.
40: quod: the antecedent of the relative pronoun is an implied id. The neuter pronoun highlights that the poet’s remains are no longer to be identified with the masculine figure.
41: tene: scansion marks this as the singular pronoun tē with the interrogative particle ne appended. The speaker addresses Tibullus (sacer uates) directly. rapuere: a syncopated form of rapuerunt.
42: pasci: the passive of pascō sometimes has a middle sense of “to feed on” with an object appearing in the ablative. timuere: a syncopated form of timuerunt, here with a complementary infinitive.
43: potuissent: a pluperfect subjunctive that highlights an unrealized possibility in the past. The plural subject is an implied eae (referring to flammae in verse 41) which is also the implied antecedent of the relative pronoun quae in verse 44.
44: urere: a complementary infinitive that is transitive. sustinuere: a syncopated form of sustinuerunt.
45: uultus: the noun is often used in the plural to mean “face” Erycis: genitive of Eryx, a mountain in Sicily known for the temple of Venus atop it. quae: the entire relative clause (modifying an implied ea) is the subject of auertit.
46: sunt: render existentially (e.g., “there are people” or “people exist”) negant: the relative clause contains an indirect statement whose accusative subject is to be supplied from context. (One can imagine an eam referring to Venus.) The indicative verb in the relative clause reveals a greater degree of assurance in the existence of specific people who believe Venus has shed tears. (Contrast the relative clause of characteristic in v. 18.)
47: Supply a form of esse as the main verb. Phaeacia tellus: see the remarks on verse 33, above.
48: ignotum: an adjective aptly leaving the noun it implicitly modifies (i.e., Tibullus) unstated. supposuisset: pluperfect subjunctive in the protasis of a past contrafactual conditional, the apodosis of which is elided.
49: hic: “here,” as opposed to in Phaeacia. The deictic adverb picks up hoc from verse 47. fugientis: a substantive adjective from the participle of fugiō = “the one departing” or “the one fleeing [life].” OLD s.v. fugio 8.
50: in cineres: the preposition in may imply that his mother brought gifts (e.g., herbs or oils) that were cremated with his body.
51: hic: “here.” See the remarks on verse 49, above. in partem … doloris: Tibullus’s sister also had a share or “part” in the grief. OLD s.v. pars 8c.
53: tuis: the adjective functions substantively to refer to Tibullus’s relatives (i.e., “and with your family”). sua: modifies oscula in the next verse. prior: a substantive adjective referring to Delia, Tibullus’s former (i.e., from his first book) love interest.
54: destituere: a syncopated form of destituerunt. rogos: a poetic plural (cf. v. 28). The singular is used in verse 6.
55: amata: enjambment separates the two components of the verb.
56: tibi: a dative of agent with a perfect passive verb. Delia addresses Tibullus directly, but, as Nemesis’s response confirms, she includes some barbs for her rival.
57: cui: Nemesis addresses Delia, rather than Tibullus. quid: “why” mea damna: Nemesis claims the losses as hers (and, implicitly, hers alone) dolori: a dative of purpose.
58: moriens: a substantive adjective from the participle of morior. The verse quotes elements of Tibullus 1.1.60.
59: e nobis: with his use of a first-person plural pronoun, the speaker makes this philosophical speculation a broader consideration of what remains “of us” after death. As the following couplets suggest, he may even be thinking expressly of “us poets.”
60: in Elysia ualle: Elysium, home to blessed people in the afterlife, is here presented as a valley. Elysium is typically populated with military figures, so the inclusion of poets in this passage may be noteworthy. Tibullus’s ontological status in Elysium, if not as a shade (umbra, v. 59), is unclear. What would seem to remain after death, if not a name (i.e., a reputation) and a shade, is a body (corpus). Might this also be the materiality of a poetic corpus?
61: obuius + dat. = “in the way of [dat.]” uenias: an optative subjunctive with a new subject clarified in the next verse. hedera: scansion shows this to be ablative. iuuenalia / tempora: scansion shows these to be accusative plural and so accusatives of respect. The dates traditionally ascribed to Catullus’s life and death (c. 84 BCE to 54 BCE) indicate that he died young.
62: Caluo … tuo: Gaius Licinius Calvus was a late Republican Roman orator and poet whom Catullus addresses as a close friend in his own poetry (e.g., c. 50). He wrote in various poetic forms, including elegy, but little of his poetry is extant. Catulle: the vocative finally indicates the subject of uenias (v. 61). Gaius Valerius Catullus was a late Republican Roman poet who also wrote in various poetic forms, including elegiac couplets. Much of his poetry survives, including a poem in which he appears to praise Calvus’s elegy for a certain Quintilia (c. 96).
63: tu: the speaker adds another addressee, revealed to be Gallus in the next verse. The predicate of the sentence is resupplied with uenias in verse 61. Ovid regarded Cornelius Gallus as the generic forebear of Tibullus, Propertius, and himself (Tristia 4.10.51-54), but the elegiac poet also garnered the attention and respect of Vergil (Eclogues 10). His commemoration of his own military successes with monuments in Egypt seems to have led him into disfavor with his “friend,” the emperor Augustus. After Augustus renounced their friendship, Gallus died by suicide in 26 BCE. For his political downfall and suicide, see Cassius Dio 53.23. temerati crimen amici: Gallus’s crime is construed as a violation of friendship. The speaker indicates that the crimen must be falsum if Gallus is to find himself in Elysium.
64: sanguinis atque animae … prodige: Gallus is “wasteful” of his blood and life because, assuming his crime wasn’t authentic, his suicide was unneccesary.
65: The speaker resumes his address of Tibullus. comes + dat. = “companion to [dat.]” si … modo: similar in sense to dummodo. You could render this as “if … really” qua: construe with the sense of aliqua.
67: ossa quieta: vocative tuta: scansion shows this to be ablative with urna. The adjective is another potential “transferred epithet,” although if the urn is safe, so too, it seems, would be the bones within.
68: sit: a jussive subjunctive after the imperative in the previous verse. onerosa: this adjective often takes a dative of reference
